issues.title: createScopedClient proxy breaks TypeScript type safety and misses auth/storage/functions namespaces
issues.description: createScopedClient returns DbClient but the Proxy only intercepts `from`. Callers using `client.auth`, `client.storage`, or `client.functions` pass through unscoped. Additionally, the TERMINAL_OPS proxy casts through `any` repeatedly, destroying type inference for all downstream query builders. The `projectScopedQuery` function referenced in the doc comment is truncated and likely missing from the file.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/client.ts, in the createScopedClient function: BEFORE: The Proxy only intercepts the `from` property and uses `any` casts on terminal operations (select/insert/update/delete/upsert), breaking TypeScript inference. The inner proxy's get handler casts `qbTarget as any` and the return as `any`, so callers lose all column type safety. AFTER: 1. Add explicit return type annotation `createScopedClient(client: DbClient, projectId: string): DbClient`. 2. In the inner proxy get handler, replace `(qbTarget as any)[qbProp](...args) as any` with a properly typed call: cast `qbProp` to `keyof typeof qbTarget`, invoke it, and only then chain `.eq('project_id', projectId)`. 3. Add a guard so that tables known to NOT have `project_id` (like `organizations`, `organization_members`) are excluded from the auto-scoping — maintain a `const UNSCOPED_TABLES = new Set(['organizations', 'organization_members', 'auth', 'storage'])` and skip the `.eq` injection for those. 4. Ensure the `projectScopedQuery` function is fully implemented (it appears truncated) — it should accept a table name and projectId, call `assertProjectAccess`, build the scoped client, invoke the callback, and use `dedup` for caching. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: transformSupabaseError is imported from two different paths causing potential runtime mismatch
issues.description: projects.ts imports `transformSupabaseError` from `'../client'` while errors.ts defines and exports it from `'../errors'`. If client.ts re-exports it, it works, but if the re-export is missing or the truncated client.ts does not include it, this will be a runtime import error. The file also imports `supabase` from `'../client'` but the actual client.ts exports are truncated making the import chain fragile.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/projects.ts, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The file has `import { supabase, transformSupabaseError } from '../client'` which assumes client.ts re-exports transformSupabaseError. This is fragile because client.ts may not re-export it (the file is truncated). AFTER: Change the import to `import { supabase } from '../client'` and `import { transformSupabaseError } from '../errors'` on a separate line. This makes the dependency explicit and avoids relying on a potentially missing re-export. Also verify that the `ApiError` import comes from `'../errors'` not `'../client'`. Scan the entire file and ensure every imported symbol resolves to a concrete export. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: No real-time subscription hooks exist in the API layer for any entity
issues.description: The Backend Architecture spec requires real-time subscriptions on rfis, daily_logs, punch_list_items, notifications, activity_feed, schedule_activities, meetings, and drawing_markups. The API client layer has zero subscription helpers. The spec's useSupabaseRealtime hook pattern is not implemented anywhere in the provided code. Without this, no page can show live updates from other users.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/client.ts, make these specific changes: BEFORE: The file contains only query/mutation helpers (supabaseQuery, supabaseMutation, createScopedClient) with no real-time subscription support. AFTER: Add a `subscribeToTable` utility function at the bottom of the file: `export function subscribeToTable(table: string, projectId: string, event: '*' | 'INSERT' | 'UPDATE' | 'DELETE', onPayload: (payload: { eventType: string; new: Record<string, unknown>; old: Record<string, unknown> }) => void): { unsubscribe: () => void }`. Implementation: create a channel via `supabase.channel(`realtime:${table}:${projectId}`)`, call `.on('postgres_changes', { event, schema: 'public', table, filter: `project_id=eq.${projectId}` }, onPayload)`, call `.subscribe()`, and return `{ unsubscribe: () => { supabase.removeChannel(channel) } }`. Also export a `subscribeToUserNotifications` function that subscribes to the notifications table filtered by `user_id` instead of `project_id`. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: getPortfolioMetrics and getMetrics lack loading/error boundary integration and make unbounded parallel queries
issues.description: getPortfolioMetrics calls `supabase.rpc('get_portfolio_metrics')` but the file is truncated — the response handling, fallback on missing RPC, and the two parallel count queries are not visible. If the RPC does not exist in the database, this will throw an unhandled error with no fallback. The dedupTtl wrapper helps but the inner function has no try/catch for partial failures of the parallel queries.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/organizations.ts, in the getPortfolioMetrics function: BEFORE: The function calls `supabase.rpc('get_portfolio_metrics', { org_id: orgId })` and throws on error, but has no fallback if the RPC function does not exist in the database (which is likely since no migration file was provided). The function is also truncated so the parallel count queries and return statement are missing. AFTER: 1. Wrap the entire body of the dedupTtl callback in a try/catch. 2. If the RPC call fails with code '42883' (function does not exist), fall back to a manual aggregation: query `projects` table for the org, then run parallel counts on rfis/punch_items scoped to those project IDs. 3. Ensure the function always returns a valid PortfolioMetrics object even on partial failure — use zero-value defaults: `{ totalProjects: 0, activeProjects: 0, totalContractValue: 0, avgCompletionPercentage: 0, openRfis: 0, overdueRfis: 0, openPunchItems: 0, scheduleVarianceDays: 0 }`. 4. Complete the truncated return statement so the function compiles. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

issues.title: assertProjectAccess and assertOrganizationAccess middleware may not enforce RLS on the client side
issues.description: people.ts calls `assertProjectAccess(projectId)` before queries, but this middleware is imported from `'../middleware/projectScope'` which is not provided. If this function only checks a local store (not the database), a compromised client could bypass it. Additionally, the queries in getCrews, getDirectory, getMeetings use `supabase` directly (not createScopedClient), so the project_id filter is manual and could be omitted by accident in future functions.
issues.prompt: In the file src/api/endpoints/people.ts, make these specific changes: BEFORE: Functions call `assertProjectAccess(projectId)` then manually chain `.eq('project_id', projectId)` on every query. This is error-prone — a new function could forget the .eq() filter. The supabase client is imported directly. AFTER: 1. Import `createScopedClient` from `'../client'`. 2. In each exported function (getCrews, getDirectory, getMeetings, getUpcomingMeetings), after calling `assertProjectAccess(projectId)`, create `const scoped = createScopedClient(supabase, projectId)` and use `scoped.from(...)` instead of `supabase.from(...)`. This way the project_id filter is automatically injected and cannot be accidentally omitted. 3. Remove the now-redundant `.eq('project_id', projectId)` calls since createScopedClient handles it. After changes, run: npm run build to verify no TypeScript errors.

competitive_intel: Procore's API layer is mature with versioned REST endpoints, webhook delivery with retry, and full OpenAPI documentation. SiteSync's API layer has good foundational patterns (typed error hierarchy, query key factories, request deduplication, scoped client proxy) but is significantly incomplete: real-time subscriptions are absent, the scoped client proxy breaks type safety with `any` casts, several endpoint files are truncated mid-function, and the v1 router is a registration table with no actual dispatch. Fieldwire ships a simpler but fully functional offline-first sync protocol. SiteSync's offline story is unimplemented despite architecture docs describing it. The error handling and security middleware patterns are ahead of most construction SaaS but need to be connected end-to-end.
summary: The Database & API Client module has a sophisticated architecture on paper — scoped clients, typed error hierarchies, query key factories, request deduplication, and a versioned API router — but execution is incomplete. Critical gaps: real-time subscriptions are entirely missing (no entity updates propagate live), the createScopedClient proxy destroys TypeScript type safety through repeated `any` casts, several endpoint files are truncated mid-function leaving broken imports, the portfolio metrics function will crash if the expected RPC does not exist, and manual `.eq('project_id')` filters are error-prone when the scoped client could enforce them automatically. The error handling layer is the strongest part, with proper error classification and user-friendly message mapping. Security patterns exist but are not consistently applied across all endpoints. The module scores 40 overall — the architecture is sound but roughly half the implementation is missing or truncated.
